name: Vedrfolnir
description: Vedrfolnir is a hawk perched atop the head of the giant Hræsvelgr, who sits at the end of the world. Hræsvelgr flaps his wings, creating the winds that blow across the earth, and Vedrfolnir sits atop him, observing all that happens in the world below. Though relatively minor in the grand scheme of Norse mythology, Vedrfolnir represents the connection between the cosmic forces and the observation of the world. He is a symbol of awareness and the ever-present nature of the natural world. His name translates roughly to 'weather-faded' or 'wind-withered,' suggesting his constant exposure to the elements and the winds created by Hræsvelgr. Some interpretations suggest Vedrfolnir may also be a source of information for the gods, acting as a messenger or observer of events happening on Midgard. The relationship between the giant and the hawk highlights the interconnectedness of even seemingly disparate elements of the cosmos, where even a small creature like a hawk plays a role in the grand scheme of things. Vedrfolnir's existence reinforces the Norse worldview of a dynamic and interconnected universe, where even the smallest creature has a place and a purpose. He is a subtle but important part of the world's mechanics.
subtitle: Hawk atop the wind giant.
appearance: Vedrfolnir is depicted as a hawk, likely a large one, perched on the head of the giant Hræsvelgr. His feathers are described as being weathered and faded, suggesting constant exposure to the wind and elements. He is likely a bird of prey, with sharp talons and a keen gaze.
habitat: Vedrfolnir resides atop the giant Hræsvelgr, who sits at the edge of the world, specifically in Jötunheimr, the land of the giants.
behavior: Vedrfolnir is primarily an observer, watching the world below and enduring the constant winds created by Hræsvelgr. He may also act as a messenger or informant, relaying information to other entities.

keyMyths.title: Hræsvelgr and Vedrfolnir
keyMyths.description: Vedrfolnir is mentioned in the poem Vafþrúðnismál in the Poetic Edda, where he is described as sitting on the head of the giant Hræsvelgr, who causes the wind. This connection highlights the relationship between the giant and the hawk and their role in the natural world.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: A key source for Norse mythology, though Vedrfolnir is only mentioned indirectly through descriptions of Hræsvelgr.
sources.text: A key source for Norse mythology, though Vedrfolnir is only mentioned indirectly through descriptions of Hræsvelgr.

sources.author: Various
sources.description: Contains the poem Vafþrúðnismál, which directly mentions Vedrfolnir.
